title: New York Fed Quarterly Report Shows Student Loan Debt Continues to Grow

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Q1 2012 Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit showed student loan debt reached $904 billion, having grown $663 billion since 2003 and $293 billion since the household debt peak in Q3 2008. Student loans were the only form of consumer debt increasing during this period. The 90+ day delinquency rate for student loans was 8.69%, higher than mortgages, auto loans, and HELOCs.
key_points: Student loan debt reached $904 billion in Q1 2012, up $30 billion from Q4 2011
Student loans surpassed credit card debt as the second highest form of consumer debt in Q2 2010
Student loan 90+ day delinquency rate was 8.69% in Q1 2012, up from 6.13% in Q1 2003
Overall household indebtedness declined to $11.44 trillion, down $100 billion from Q4 2011
Mortgage balances fell $81 billion (1.0%) during Q1 2012
Approximately $1.06 trillion of consumer debt was delinquent, with $796 billion seriously delinquent
why_it_matters: This quarterly data release documents household deleveraging trends following the 2008 financial crisis. Student loan debt was the sole category growing while other consumer debt categories declined, indicating structural shifts in household balance sheets. Note: The archive scope question here is whether household debt/consumer credit data falls under 'market structure' as defined (trading mechanisms, market operations, plumbing) or represents a separate category.
macro_context: Historical household debt data from Q1 2012 showing consumer deleveraging trends during the post-2008 recovery period. Student loan debt was uniquely growing while other debt categories (mortgages, credit cards) were contracting since the Q3 2008 peak. The 90+ day delinquency rate for student loans (8.69%) was higher than mortgages, auto loans, and HELOCs.
